judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports are nearly identical in analysis and correctly identify the token shortfall and recommend switching to ridgeline, but Report 2 additionally flags the staleness of the usage snapshot and the need to re-verify live limits before relying on it, which Report 1 omits.

report_1.evidence: full run needs 5,200 × 1,800 = 9,360,000 tokens... northpeak ... only 640,000 remaining. That covers roughly ... 355 transcripts — about 7% of the dataset.

report_2.evidence: 5,200 transcripts × 1,800 avg tokens/call = 9,360,000 tokens needed for the full run. northpeak (primary, default path): window total 10,000,000 tokens, but only 640,000 remaining

report_1.evidence: ridgeline (secondary, config/providers.yaml:7-11): window total 12,000,000 tokens, 10,800,000 remaining — enough to cover the full 9,360,000-token run

report_2.evidence: ridgeline (secondary): window total 12,000,000 tokens, with 10,800,000 remaining. That comfortably covers the 9,360,000 tokens needed

report_1.evidence: I would not run run_nightly.sh/score_batch.py as-is with its implicit default. I'd explicitly pass --provider secondary (i.e., target ridgeline/atlas-mini) instead of relying on the primary/northpeak default

report_2.evidence: I would not run the default command as-is. I'd launch with the secondary backend explicitly: python3 scripts/score_batch.py --dataset data/manifest.json --provider secondary

Before actually kicking it off I'd also re-verify the usage snapshot rather than trust it blindly — it's about 3 hours old (generated 19:25 UTC vs. "now" 22:30 UTC) and isn't flagged as fresh, so ridgeline's true remaining balance at launch time could be somewhat different from 10.8M if anything else has been drawing on that window since the snapshot was taken.
